title: Beyond a Single Number: Evaluating Quantized Models for Deployment
excerpt: A three-part series developing ByteShape's practical approach to quantized-model evaluation: does the model fit, how well does it perform, and how fast does it run on the target hardware?
content_html:
Quantized models are often compared using a single convenient number: model size, bits per weight, perplexity, KL divergence, or tokens per second.

Each of these metrics answers a useful question. None answers the full deployment question:

Which quant should I run on my hardware?

This three-part series develops ByteShape’s practical approach to quantized-model evaluation. The framework is simple:

  1. Does the model fit?
  2. How well does it perform?
  3. How fast does it run on the target hardware?

Fit determines which models are possible. Downstream quality and measured throughput determine which of those models are practical.

Part 1: Fit, Quality, and Speed: A Practical Framework for Choosing Quants

Why fit, downstream quality, and throughput matter more than any single proxy.

Part 1 introduces the evaluation framework. It separates three categories of measurement:

  • fit metrics, such as model size, BPW, and context-memory requirements;
  • quality measurements, such as downstream task benchmarks;
  • deployment measurements, such as token-generation throughput on the target hardware.

It also distinguishes downstream quality from fidelity to a reference model. Perplexity and KL divergence are useful for detecting substantial degradation, but they do not directly measure whether a quant answers correctly, follows instructions, or writes working code.

Read Part 1 →

Part 2: Displacement Is Not Direction: Why Fidelity Metrics Fail to Rank Near-Baseline Quants

Testing perplexity, KLD, and related diagnostics on real quantized models.

Part 2 moves from intuition to evidence.

Across the full model cohort, perplexity and KLD appear to predict benchmark quality. But that relationship is largely driven by clearly degraded quants. Among models that remain close to baseline quality, the ranking signal collapses.

We test 14 fidelity-metric variants across different datasets, context lengths, aggregation methods, and response-only scoring setups. The result remains consistent: fidelity metrics can detect large departures from BF16, but they do not reliably rank near-baseline quants.

At the prompt level, KLD tells us how often a quant differs from BF16. It does not tell us whether those differences help, hurt, or leave the outcome unchanged.

KLD measures displacement. Benchmark quality depends on the direction of that displacement.

Read Part 2 →

Part 3: Size Is Not Speed: Why BPW Fails to Rank Similar-Sized Quants

Why model size and bits per weight do not reliably rank similar-sized quants.

Part 3 examines the deployment side of the problem.

Model size and bits per weight are useful for determining whether a quant fits and for estimating broad performance trends. But among similarly sized models, BPW does not reliably predict measured token-generation speed.

Actual throughput also depends on:

  • quantization format,
  • tensor shapes and group sizes,
  • kernel support,
  • memory-access behaviour,
  • CPU and GPU architecture,
  • workload and context length,
  • and inference-engine implementation.

A smaller quant may be slower than a larger one. A format that performs well on one GPU may rank differently on another device.

BPW measures storage cost. It does not directly measure realized speed.

Read Part 3 →

Proxy rank is not deployment rank

Parts 2 and 3 reveal the same underlying problem.

KLD is a proxy for retained fidelity to the BF16 reference. BPW is a proxy for model size and, indirectly, expected speed. Both are useful screening metrics. Neither directly measures the final deployment outcome.

The figure below compares where each quant is placed by those proxies with where it lands using measured deployment results on the RTX 6000.

Each quant appears twice:

  • a hollow circle marks its proxy-ranked position, where lower KLD implies better quality and lower BPW implies greater speed;
  • an arrowhead marks its measured position, where downstream benchmark score determines quality rank and measured token-generation throughput determines speed rank.

An arrow connects the two positions.

If the proxies reproduced the deployment ranking, the hollow circle and arrowhead would overlap. Instead, many arrows are long, and their directions vary substantially.

Vertical movement represents disagreement between KLD and benchmark-quality.

Horizontal movement represents disagreement between BPW and measured-throughput.

The total displacement combines both ranking errors.

Some quants move vertically because their benchmark-quality rank differs from their KLD-based proxy rank. Others move horizontally because their quantization format runs faster or slower than BPW predicts. Some move substantially on both axes.

The exact throughput ranking is hardware-specific. The figure uses RTX 6000 measurements, but the broader conclusion is general:

The quant that wins the proxies is often not the quant that wins in deployment.

The practical takeaway

A practical quant-selection process has three steps:

  1. Filter by fit. Eliminate models that exceed the available memory at the required context length.
  2. Compare downstream quality. Benchmark the remaining models on tasks that reflect the intended workload.
  3. Measure throughput. Test the actual inference engine, hardware, context length, and generation settings.

The best choice is the quant that fits, performs well, and runs fast enough for the intended workload.

How fast does it run on the target hardware? Fit determines which models are possible. Downstream quality and measured throughput determine which of those models are practical. It also distinguishes downstream quality from fidelity to a reference model. The result remains consistent: fidelity metrics can detect large departures from BF16, but they do not reliably rank near-baseline quants. Benchmark quality depends on the direction of that displacement. blocks.html_en: It also distinguishes downstream quality from fidelity to a reference model. Perplexity and KL divergence are useful for detecting substantial degradation, but they do not directly measure whether a quant answers correctly, follows instructions, or writes working code.

blocks.html_en: Across the full model cohort, perplexity and KLD appear to predict benchmark quality. But that relationship is largely driven by clearly degraded quants. Among models that remain close to baseline quality, the ranking signal collapses.

sentences.zh: 在整个模型队列中，困惑度和 KLD 似乎可以预测基准质量。

sentences.en: But that relationship is largely driven by clearly degraded quants.
sentences.zh: 但这种关系很大程度上是由明显退化的量化分析师驱动的。

sentences.en: Among models that remain close to baseline quality, the ranking signal collapses.
sentences.zh: 在仍接近基线质量的模型中，排名信号崩溃。

blocks.html_en: We test 14 fidelity-metric variants across different datasets, context lengths, aggregation methods, and response-only scoring setups. The result remains consistent: fidelity metrics can detect large departures from BF16, but they do not reliably rank near-baseline quants.

sentences.zh: 我们在不同的数据集、上下文长度、聚合方法和仅响应评分设置中测试了 14 种保真度度量变体。

sentences.en: The result remains consistent: fidelity metrics can detect large departures from BF16, but they do not reliably rank near-baseline quants.
sentences.zh: 结果保持一致：保真度指标可以检测到与 BF16 的较大偏差，但它们不能可靠地对接近基线的量化进行排名。

blocks.html_en: At the prompt level, KLD tells us how often a quant differs from BF16. It does not tell us whether those differences help, hurt, or leave the outcome unchanged.

sentences.en: At the prompt level, KLD tells us how often a quant differs from BF16.
sentences.zh: 在提示级别，KLD 告诉我们量化指标与 BF16 不同的频率。

sentences.en: It does not tell us whether those differences help, hurt, or leave the outcome unchanged.
sentences.zh: 它没有告诉我们这些差异是否有助于、损害或使结果保持不变。

sentences.en: Benchmark quality depends on the direction of that displacement.
sentences.zh: 基准质量取决于位移的方向。

blocks.html_en: • a hollow circle marks its proxy-ranked position, where lower KLD implies better quality and lower BPW implies greater speed;

sentences.zh: 一些量化分析师会垂直移动，因为他们的基准质量排名与基于 KLD 的代理排名不同。

sentences.en: Others move horizontally because their quantization format runs faster or slower than BPW predicts.
sentences.zh: 其他人则水平移动，因为它们的量化格式运行得比 BPW 预测的更快或更慢。

blocks.html_en: The quant that wins the proxies is often not the quant that wins in deployment.

sentences.en: The quant that wins the proxies is often not the quant that wins in deployment.
sentences.zh: 赢得代理的量化分析师通常不是赢得部署的量化分析师。

blocks.html_en: The practical takeaway

sentences.en: The practical takeaway
sentences.zh: 实用的外卖

blocks.html_en: A practical quant-selection process has three steps:

sentences.en: A practical quant-selection process has three steps:
sentences.zh: 实际的定量选择过程分为三个步骤：

blocks.html_en: • Filter by fit. Eliminate models that exceed the available memory at the required context length.

sentences.en: Filter by fit.
sentences.zh: 按适合度过滤。

sentences.en: Eliminate models that exceed the available memory at the required context length.
sentences.zh: 消除在所需上下文长度下超出可用内存的模型。

blocks.html_en: • Compare downstream quality. Benchmark the remaining models on tasks that reflect the intended workload.

sentences.en: Compare downstream quality.
sentences.zh: 比较下游质量。

sentences.en: Benchmark the remaining models on tasks that reflect the intended workload.
sentences.zh: 对反映预期工作负载的任务的其余模型进行基准测试。

blocks.html_en: • Measure throughput. Test the actual inference engine, hardware, context length, and generation settings.

sentences.en: Measure throughput.
sentences.zh: 测量吞吐量。

sentences.en: Test the actual inference engine, hardware, context length, and generation settings.
sentences.zh: 测试实际的推理引擎、硬件、上下文长度和生成设置。

blocks.html_en: The best choice is the quant that fits, performs well, and runs fast enough for the intended workload.

sentences.en: The best choice is the quant that fits, performs well, and runs fast enough for the intended workload.
sentences.zh: 最好的选择是适合预期工作负载、性能良好且运行速度足够快的量化工具。

content_html_zh:
量化模型通常使用一个方便的数字进行比较：模型大小、每权重位数、复杂度、KL 散度或每秒令牌数。

这些指标中的每一个都回答了一个有用的问题。 没有人回答完整的部署问题：

我应该在我的硬件上运行哪个定量分析？

这个由三部分组成的系列开发了 ByteShape 量化模型评估的实用方法。 框架很简单：

  1. 模型合适吗？
  2. 它的表现如何？
  3. 它在目标硬件上运行的速度有多快？

适合度决定了哪些模型是可能的。 下游质量和测量的吞吐量决定了哪些模型是实用的。

第 1 部分：适合、质量和速度：选择量化的实用框架

为什么适合、下游质量和吞吐量比任何单一代理更重要。

第 1 部分介绍评估框架。 它将测量分为三类：

  • 拟合指标，例如模型大小、BPW 和上下文内存要求；
  • 质量测量，例如下游任务基准；
  • 部署测量，例如目标硬件上的令牌生成吞吐量。

它还区分下游质量与参考模型的保真度。 困惑度和 KL 散度对于检测实质性退化很有用，但它们不能直接衡量量化人员是否正确回答、遵循指令或编写工作代码。

阅读第 1 部分 →

第 2 部分：位移不是方向：为什么保真度指标无法对接近基线的量化进行排名

在真实量化模型上测试困惑度、KLD 和相关诊断。

第 2 部分从直觉转向证据。

在整个模型队列中，困惑度和 KLD 似乎可以预测基准质量。 但这种关系很大程度上是由明显退化的量化分析师驱动的。 在仍接近基线质量的模型中，排名信号崩溃。

我们在不同的数据集、上下文长度、聚合方法和仅响应评分设置中测试了 14 种保真度度量变体。 结果保持一致：保真度指标可以检测到与 BF16 的较大偏差，但它们不能可靠地对接近基线的量化进行排名。

在提示级别，KLD 告诉我们量化指标与 BF16 不同的频率。 它没有告诉我们这些差异是否有助于、损害或使结果保持不变。

KLD 测量位移。 基准质量取决于位移的方向。

阅读第 2 部分 →

第 3 部分：规模不是速度：为什么 BPW 无法对规模相似的量化进行排名

为什么模型大小和每权重位数不能可靠地对相似大小的量化进行排名。

第 3 部分研究问题的部署方面。

模型大小和每权重位数对于确定量化是否适合以及估计广泛的性能趋势非常有用。 但在类似大小的模型中，BPW 无法可靠地预测测量到的令牌生成速度。

实际吞吐量还取决于：

  • 量化格式，
  • 张量形状和组大小，
  • 内核支持，
  • 内存访问行为，
  • CPU和GPU架构，
  • 工作量和上下文长度，
  • 和推理引擎的实现。

较小的数量可能比较大的数量慢。 在一个 GPU 上表现良好的格式在另一设备上的排名可能会有所不同。

BPW 衡量存储成本。 它不直接测量实现的速度。

阅读第 3 部分 →

代理级别不是部署级别

第 2 部分和第 3 部分揭示了相同的根本问题。

KLD 是对 BF16 参考保留保真度的代表。 BPW 代表模型大小，并间接代表预期速度。 两者都是有用的筛选指标。 两者都没有直接衡量最终的部署结果。

下图将这些代理放置每个量化的位置与使用 RTX 6000 上测量的部署结果得出的位置进行了比较。

每个量化出现两次：

  • 空心圆圈标记其代理排名位置，其中较低的 KLD 意味着更好的质量，较低的 BPW 意味着更高的速度；
  • 箭头标记其测量位置，下游基准分数决定质量排名，测量的代币生成吞吐量决定速度排名。

箭头连接两个位置。

如果代理重现部署排名，空心圆圈和箭头将重叠。 相反，许多箭头很长，而且它们的方向变化很大。

垂直移动代表 KLD 与基准质量之间的分歧。

水平移动代表 BPW 和测量吞吐量之间的不一致。

总位移结合了两个排名误差。

一些量化分析师会垂直移动，因为他们的基准质量排名与基于 KLD 的代理排名不同。 其他人则水平移动，因为它们的量化格式运行得比 BPW 预测的更快或更慢。 有些在两个轴上都有很大的移动。

确切的吞吐量排名取决于硬件。 该图使用 RTX 6000 测量结果，但更广泛的结论是一般性的：

赢得代理的量化分析师通常不是赢得部署的量化分析师。

实用的外卖

实际的定量选择过程分为三个步骤：

  1. 按适合度过滤。 消除在所需上下文长度下超出可用内存的模型。
  2. 比较下游质量。 对反映预期工作负载的任务的其余模型进行基准测试。
  3. 测量吞吐量。 测试实际的推理引擎、硬件、上下文长度和生成设置。

最好的选择是适合预期工作负载、性能良好且运行速度足够快的量化工具。
